sentences.text: I did not finish the story there.
We went out in that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ever-smaller--
Jewish-- Jewish neighborhoods that was left of the ghetto in Gorlice.
And soon thereafter, all the young and vigorous people were loaded into cattle cars-- and that is-- was another experience-- and shipped to Plaszow.
Now, a very small contingent of Jewish people remained in town.
They were the ones that were highly positioned.
And considered-- yes, they remained in town.
My brother and my mother remained in town because they were [? both ?] working in the general hospital.
And we actually thought that they could escape this murderous orgy that was unleashed upon us.
But we were taken away, along with the rest of the people, except for this small contingent of Jews that were higher positioned.
And we were packed into cattle cars, 100 men in a-- in a car.
There was-- the Germans offered no provisions, but we had some food stuff with us.
There were no provisions for hygiene or toilet service.
Just one corner was made for bowel function.
And as far as urination or so, it was done through the cracks of the cattle cars.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] It was a horrifying experience.
Along the road, there were such who succumbed to the heat, exhaustion, hunger.
They-- they were simply made into mattresses.
We-- we were resting our head on their bodies.

But the trip-- even though it is a very short trip from Gorlice to Plaszow, it took, to the best of my recollections, three days, because the human cargo had two things-- it was transported in a sort of clandestine manner, not to make too much noise with it, and it had a low priority.
In those days, every German locomotive carried a big sign, [GERMAN].
Wheels must keep rolling for the victory.
The Germans considered all transports of military assets to have priority.
The human cargo was of low priority.
And so our short trip took three days-- enough to kill many.
But once we got to Plaszow, we were unloaded.
Everything was taken away from us, everything that was personal.
If it was a letter-- so everything was taken away.
Everything.
The clothing, the personal belongings.
I-- I had some valuables.
Everything was taken away from us.
We were shaved.
We were-- we were-- everything was shaved-- body hair.
And we were what they called [GERMAN]-- deloused.
They had a special procedure for that.
Showered, and so-- and we received [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the kind of clothing-- I hate to call it uniforms-- such-- such identify the prisoner with-- with the sign of Jude, that is a Jew.
That was as a protection, apparently, so we cannot escape because that we are easily identifiable.
And we were assigned to hard labor.
My father and I, we were assigned to the railroad-- to build a railroad.
That involved hard labor, and it included loading and unloading of railroad cars of supplies.
One interesting part of this-- of this activity was that-- that in winter of 1941, '42, we already saw the Germans unloading the soldiers that were frozen.
They-- they-- they came from the eastern front, not with bullet wounds, but with frozen noses, and teeth, and fingers, and feet, and were unloaded.
We could observe this.
We didn't do the unloading, but we could observe it.
They were not dead.
They were just badly--
--injured by freezing.
Frozen.
But-- but our-- out activity was building the railroads and unloading railroad cars-- freight, all kinds of freight, that the Germans needed.
The camp was led by an SS officer by the name of Miller-- Miller.
Miller was fairly benign as the Germans-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in general-- as you would judge the SS at that time.

But he, too, was under obligation to present some brutality because apparently, this was meant as a way of maintaining an atmosphere of fear that-- that lends itself much better to control of the population.
So periodically, we would have hangings.
They would find a reason.
The main reason was an attempt to escape.
That was invariably under death penalty.
They would call out all the prisoners early in the morning, maybe 5 o'clock.
It was especially bad on winter days.
We were not very well clothed.
They would all be put-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they would all be assembled in that square in the camp, and would witness the hanging.
Those that were hanged remained there for days so that everybody can see it and remember it.
Brutality, by the way, was not something the Germans were hiding.
It was something they were advertising because it spread fear, and fear lent itself much better to control.
In fact, Miller had periodic hangings for that purpose.
But we know that some of it was pressure from-- from the leadership of the SS, because there were commissions coming to review the camps.
When they came, there was invariably some kind of brutality staged.
But beatings or other harassments, in general, were very few, very few under Miller.
But Miller's camp was the first one to close, and when Miller's camp closed, we were taken up to the [NON-ENGLISH].
That is that infamous camp that is represented in that movie.
The-- the-- the commander, the commandant, of this camp was a man by the name of Goeth.
He was truly sub-human.
He was a murderous character.
When we came to Goeth, we were lined up, and they were counting one, two, three.
My father was one, I was two, and a boy behind me that I knew well, a wonderful Jewish boy from Gorlice, was three.
Everybody who was three was pulled aside.
We were-- we were made to understand that there is not enough place for all of us, so one third of us will have to be liquidated.
They were taken aside-- it was purely by one, two, three-- they were taken aside, and they had-- you must forgive me.
I know you speak Polish, [POLISH].
This was a place-- it is a derogatory place of a hill that they used for killings.
And we heard the machine guns killing these people right away.
So every third one was taken out of the-- as we came into this murderous camp by-- again, the main killing was done by the Ukrainians, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] under German supervision.
Of course, the Germans gave them the orders and supervised them.
In any event, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] every third one was taken out and murdered by machine gun fire.
We could hear the machine gun fire.
We could not see the action, but we were soon told where it took place and how it took place by those who were in the camp and who knew that this is routine at the [NON-ENGLISH] in-- in Goeth's camp.
We spend over there only a few days.
I cannot be sure whether it was six or seven days, and we were shipped out to Skarzysko-Kamienna.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Skarzysko-Kamienna was a munitions factory of the Hassock enterprise.
Hassock was the name.
It had three divisions, A, B, and C.
We were in division A.
Division C was a chemical division, where they mixed the chemicals needed for explosives for the ammunition.
They had an explosive that they called [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] was the name.

It was severely hepatotoxic, and people who-- not only did the [NON-ENGLISH] look yellow in itself, the chemical, but the people, because of hepatotoxicity, turned jaundice, so they became as yellow as a chemical and died.
No precautions, of course, were taken, like masks to prevent inhalation, and so, to the people who worked there.
They-- most of them actually were women.
They worked there.
They inhaled it.
They had no protection whatever, and they succumbed to it.
But those who died were as yellow as the [NON-ENGLISH] itself.
Now, amongst the people who worked in-- in-- in-- in that division C was Ida, my cousin, that is now in Toronto that I mentioned earlier.
By-- by a miracle, she was not put into contact with [NON-ENGLISH].
She met up with us at a-- with a strange coincidence, when she went to-- to be showered and-- and [NON-ENGLISH], the way they called it.
We, of course-- we were delighted that she is still alive, and we were-- we were never losing hope of survival.
And indeed, as it happened, all three of us survived.
My father and I, we were working on the railroad again, loading and unloading.
They called it transport.
But my father had an unusual thing that-- he became an assistant to the driver of the locomotive.
Actually, what he did is he was shoveling coal in the locomotive.
But the locomotive went out of the camp into town, and it gave him a way to establish connections with the Polish people once again.
The Polish, at that point in time, had a mutual enemy with the Jews, and more and more of them were willing to be helpful, rather than to do them in.
There were cases where the Polish denounced, did in, and so.
The Germans knew how to provoke it.
They-- they promised them privileges, a pound of sugar and so, and always warned the Polish, if you aid a Jew, you are in for execution.
This is under death penalty.
So it's-- it's remarkable that-- the kind of help we got.
But my father established town connections, and because of it, we had a little more food.
Food were essential for survival because it is apparent, and is now known, that the kind of diet that was served to the Jews in slave labor camps was meant to exterminate them in time, all in itself.
Now, what was the diet?
[BACKGROUND NOISE] We got soup, but the soup-- it depended very much on who you are in camp and who you know.
Those who had some ins got at least a nourishing soup that was thicker and had some ingredients.
Most of it was garbage anyway.
It wasn't any kind of food from [INAUDIBLE].
It was-- it was whatever the Germans had as garbage was put into there.
But it had nutrients, and it kept us alive.
We also had a piece of bread and something with it very often, like a piece of margarine or a-- or a little bit of jam, and sometimes even a piece of sausage, but always of the lowest variety.
There were always rejects from the Germans.
However, they meant life.
I was working on the railroad, as well, in transport, mainly loading and unloading of freight.
But my father was with the locomotive.
This has a meaning because this contributed to our survival.
By the way, before I answer your question, being on the hunger and survival, we saw many people, particularly those with good appetites, starving rapidly of hunger and dying.
Many-- I see a number of them before my eyes.
So getting some food some way was a life-saving-- was a life-saving event.
Yes.
People who had-- who were geared towards eating, and so, who suffered enormously from hunger, they succumbed.
And I-- I saw a lot of that.
You asked the question, what--
The-- the Russians came closer and closer.
As the Russians came closer, the Germans had to liquidate the camps and run west.
I was now in Skarzysko, and when Skarzysko was liquidated, we were sent to Buchenwald, which is already in Germany, close to Weimar.
I cannot tell you a date, but I can tell you it's 1944.
The best I remember is I was not cold in Buchenwald, and so it must have been-- it was not winter.
It was 1944, probably spring.
I cannot-- I cannot be sure, but it was at the time of the Polish uprising in Russia, because people from that uprising were brought to Buchenwald together with us.
There was a nice story there at Buchenwald that I may want to tell you.
Some of it is eye-witness account, and some of it is first-hand knowledge.
They-- we were kept-- I was only about a week or so in Buchenwald in a [NON-ENGLISH].
That is a tent camp.
After that, I was shipped out to what they called an [NON-ENGLISH] commando, an outside commando, to Schlieben, where they manufactured panzerfaust.
Schlieben.
S-C-H-L-I-E-B-E-N.
Schlieben.
They manufactured panzerfaust.

Panzerfaust is-- is an effective modern anti-tank weapon. 'Faust' in German means 'fist,' and 'panzer' in German means 'armor.' A-- a fist against armor-- it was shoulder-fired and very effective, and the outfit belonged, again, to Hassock.
We were shipped there.
But while we were in-- in-- in Buchenwald, they brought in a gypsy family, a family of gypsy entertainers.
They were all kinds of entertainers.
They were music makers and-- and dancers and singers, all kinds of family.
There were elder, and there were children.
They played music.
They danced and sang.
They were a beautiful, artistic ensemble of gypsy music.
They were brought before the SS one evening.
They were given their costumes to entertain them.
It was a beautiful evening of entertainment.
We could hear their music, and in the distance, see them twirl.
But we know from those who attended the crematoria, that on the next morning-- I cannot tell why-- they were all killed and-- and-- and made to ashes.
I wasn't there to see it.
I only saw and-- some of the performance and heard it.
But those who conveyed what happened to them were eye witnesses, and-- and I, for one, believe it.
We never saw these people again.
But I remained there only for about six days.
And then, we went to Schlieben to the panzerfaust factory.
Still with my father, all the way through.
In that factory, I-- we again worked as laborers, mainly loading and unloading.
Apparently, this was our trade.
But something that may interest you that happened in Schlieben is Italy had surrendered.
Italy had done away with the fascist regime of-- of Mussolini, as you know, in 1943.
And later on, the Italians run away from the eastern front.
They were stopped by the Germans, and they were now sent to Schlieben as slave laborers.
But they were well fed.
That they were strong, young, vigorous.
They had been soldiers.
Once they came to Schlieben, they were under the same regimen that we were.
They received the same food.
They received the same punishment.
They soon organized and blew up the whole outfit.
They blew it all up at night.
The whole panzerfaust factory went kaput in-- in one night.
And my father was in-- in-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] in the factory at that time of the evening.
I was sure that I will never see my father again, because it was all aflame.
I was in the barracks that evening, but by a miraculous twist, my father survived with very little injury, and we went on to rebuild Schlieben after this blow up.
But this was already the time in history when we witnessed the end coming by the Allied air bombardment.
We could almost set our clocks as the air raids came in.

They came in exactly the same time day and night, and we could see the bombardments of the cities all over, the nearby cities aflame, and we knew then that if we hang in, we may survive-- because at that time, hunger, and hard labor, and exposure were really the only things to kill us.
The murder, the hanging, or killing, or brutality had-- had disappeared.
I don't remember any of that anymore in Schlieben.
Now, we were already in Germany.
Schlieben is in Germany, and the behavior was quite different.
But this lasted till about March of 1945.
At that point, we were loaded into wagons again, cattle cars, and we were transported to Theresienstadt.
